docs.title: Welcome to TestForge 🛠️
docs.text:
Scientifically Validated AI Test Generation

TestForge is a high-integrity CLI framework that combines Deterministic Static Analysis with Agentic AI to generate, validate, and harden Python unit test suites.

It moves beyond simple line coverage by employing McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity, Topological Dependency Resolution, and Mutation Testing to ensure tests are structurally sound and behaviorally rigorous.

docs.title: The TestForge Philosophy
docs.text:
In the era of AI-assisted coding, generating tests is easy, but generating correct and rigorous tests is hard. TestForge is built on the belief that AI should be guided by deterministic engineering principles:

  • Don't Guess, Analyze

    We use AST analysis to understand your code's structure before the AI ever sees it. No hallucinations, just symbols.

  • Complexity Matters

    McCabe metrics prioritize testing for high-risk paths. We spend your LLM tokens where they are needed most.

  • Verify the Verification

    Mutation Testing proves your tests actually work. If a test can't kill a mutant, it's not a valid engineering artifact.

docs.title: How It Works (Under the Hood)
docs.text:
TestForge operates on a continuous Research -> Plan -> Generate -> Validate lifecycle:

  1. Map: Scans the project to build a Dependency DAG and complexity map.
  2. Plan: Generates an editable execution_plan.yaml during dry-run for human review.
  3. Execute: AI generates tests following a strict "Scientific Plan" for each module.
  4. Harden: mutmut injects faults to ensure the test suite is behaviorally rigorous.

Get Started with TestForge Deep Dive: Methodology

docs.title: The Execution Dashboard & Milestones
docs.text: During a run-all execution, TestForge displays a real-time terminal dashboard powered by rich. This dashboard provides high-level visibility into the pipeline's progress and scientific health.

docs.title: Milestone Definitions
docs.text:
We track three specific milestones to ensure the generated test suite is robust:

  • M1 Core >= 80%: Triggered when at least 80% of the testable modules have a "completed" status in the ledger. This indicates the primary logic of the project is covered.
  • M2 P0 >= 90%: Triggered when 90% of modules are validated. This typically covers all critical path components.
  • M3 Suite Stable: Triggered only when 100% of modules are validated and there are zero failed modules in the ledger.

docs.title: Editable Execution Planning (YAML Overrides)
docs.text: TestForge follows a "Plan-First" philosophy. By using the --dry-run flag with run-all, you can generate an execution_plan.yaml file that allows you to manually control the pipeline before any LLM tokens are spent.

docs.title: Why use an Execution Plan?
docs.text:
1. Context Tuning: Remove large, noisy files from a module's context to reduce costs.
  2. Custom Ordering: Reorder the pipeline if you have specific testing priorities.
  3. Output Redirection: Manually change where the generated test files will be saved.

docs.title: Sample execution_plan.yaml
docs.text:
execution_order:
  - src/testforge/utils/env_setup.py
  - src/testforge/core/context.py
phases:
  Phase_1:
    module: src/testforge/utils/env_setup.py
    context_files:
      - src/testforge/utils/env_setup.py
    expected_output: tests/test_env_setup.py
  Phase_2:
    module: src/testforge/core/context.py
    context_files:
      - src/testforge/core/context.py
      - src/testforge/utils/env_setup.py  # Dependency added by graph logic
    expected_output: tests/test_context.py

Manual Override

To use your custom plan, simply run testforge run-all . without the --dry-run flag. TestForge will detect the YAML file and use it as the source of truth for the execution sequence.

docs.title: The Execution Ledger (State Management)
docs.text:
TestForge maintains a persistent state file at .testforge/ledger.json in your project root.

Under the Hood: Ledger Schema

The ledger tracks every module by its relative path: * Status: completed: Module passed pytest and mutation tests. It will be skipped in future runs. * Status: failed: Module failed validation or hit the retry limit. * Error: Stores the last traceback or error message for failed modules.

To ignore the ledger and restart the entire pipeline, use the --force flag.

docs.title: Dynamic Model Routing
docs.text:
You can optimize costs and performance by routing different phases to different models.

CLI OverrideEnvironment Variables 
testforge run-all . --mapper-model gpt-4o-mini --planner-model o1-preview --coder-model gpt-4o

export LLM_MAPPER_MODEL="gpt-4o-mini"
export LLM_PLANNER_MODEL="o1-preview"
export LLM_CODER_MODEL="gpt-4o"

docs.title: Template Overrides
docs.text:
Every LLM interaction is based on Jinja2 templates. You can customize these to enforce your company's coding standards.

  1. Create a folder named .testforge/templates/ in your project root.
  2. Copy any default template from the TestForge source (e.g., plan_tests.j2).
  3. Modify the copy. TestForge will now prioritize your local version.

docs.title: CLI Reference 🛠️
docs.text: Detailed documentation for the TestForge command-line interface. Each command is designed to be deterministic and transparent.

docs.title: testforge map
docs.text:
Builds the architectural topography of the codebase.

testforge map [PATH] [OPTIONS]

Arguments: - PATH: Path to the codebase to analyze.

Options: - --output PATH: Output file for the architecture map. (Default: architecture.md) - --mapper-model TEXT: LLM model for the mapping phase. - --dry-run: Estimate tokens and cost without running AI.

Under the Hood: Mapping Logic

  1. File Discovery: Scans for all .py files, respecting .gitignore.
  2. AST Analysis: Extracts classes, functions, and docstrings.
  3. Cross-Reference: Matches internal imports to create the dependency mapping.
  4. Markdown Generation: Formats the ModuleInfo objects into a human-readable architecture.md.

docs.title: testforge run-all
docs.text:
The full iterative pipeline (Bottom-Up) on the entire codebase.

testforge run-all [PATH] [OPTIONS]

Arguments: - PATH: Project root directory.

Options: - --mapper-model TEXT: LLM model for the mapping phase. - --planner-model TEXT: LLM model for the planning phase. - --coder-model TEXT: LLM model for the coding phase. - --force: Force run by clearing the execution ledger and ignore execution_plan.yaml. - --dry-run: Perform a full simulation and generate an editable execution plan.

Under the Hood: The Master Pipeline

  1. Dependency Sort: Builds the DAG and calculates the topological processing order.
  2. Heuristic Filtering: Skips logic-less modules (0 classes/functions).
  3. Execution Plan:
    • If --dry-run: Generates execution_plan.yaml and displays a detailed token/context report.
    • If Standard Run: Checks for execution_plan.yaml. If found, it uses the user-modified order and context.
  4. State Management: Updates .testforge/ledger.json after every module to enable "Pause and Resume" capability.
  5. Scientific Loop: For each module: Plan -> Generate -> Pytest -> Mutmut Hardening.
  6. Reporting: Displays the final visual dashboard with Milestone status.

docs.title: testforge ci
docs.text:
CI/CD Headless Mode: Generate tests only for modified files and their dependents.

testforge ci [OPTIONS]

Options: - --project-root PATH: Project root directory. - --base-branch TEXT: Base branch to diff against. (Default: main) - --dry-run: Output topological order without running.

Under the Hood: Change Analysis

  1. Git Diff: Uses GitPython to identify files changed between the current HEAD and the base-branch.
  2. Impact Analysis: Consults the dependency graph to find all modules that import the changed files (downstream dependents).
  3. Targeted Run: Executes the run-all logic only on this subset of modules, ensuring PRs are verified without re-testing the entire codebase.

docs.title: testforge generate
docs.text:
Generates and validates a unit test suite for a single file.

testforge generate [FILE_PATH] [OPTIONS]

Arguments: - FILE_PATH: The file to generate tests for.

Options: - --project-root PATH: Project root directory. - --planner-model TEXT: LLM model for the planning phase. - --coder-model TEXT: LLM model for the coding phase. - --dry-run: Estimate tokens and cost without running AI.

Under the Hood: Single-File Logic

  1. Isolation: Clears the AI context to prevent "leaking" information from previous files.
  2. Deterministic Preview: Summarizes the module's complexity and interface before calling the LLM.
  3. Self-Healing Loop: Runs up to 3 repair attempts if pytest fails, feeding the traceback directly back to the AI.

docs.title: testforge refactor-fixtures
docs.text:
Scans test files and elevates local fixtures to conftest.py.

testforge refactor-fixtures [OPTIONS]

Options: - --project-root PATH: Project root directory. - --coder-model TEXT: LLM model for the refactoring phase.

Under the Hood: Global Elevation

  1. Discovery: Recursively scans the tests/ directory for all files matching test_*.py.
  2. State Initialization: Ensures a tests/conftest.py file exists to act as the destination.
  3. Cross-File Analysis: Loads all test files and the conftest.py into the LLM's context.
  4. Deduplication: The AI identifies @pytest.fixture definitions that are duplicated or highly similar across multiple files.
  5. Refactoring:
    • Extracts the fixtures to conftest.py.
    • Removes local definitions from individual test files.
    • Updates imports and references to ensure tests remain functional and "dry".

docs.title: Contributing to TestForge 🤝
docs.text: We welcome contributions from the community! Whether you're fixing bugs, adding new features, or improving documentation, your help is appreciated.

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository: 
    git clone https://github.com/BMike10/testforge/
cd testforge
  2. Install in editable mode with development dependencies: 
    pip install -e ".[docs]"
  3. Run tests: 
    pytest

docs.title: Coding Standards
docs.text:
• Use Type Hints for all function signatures.
  • Adhere to PEP 8 styling.
  • Ensure all new features are covered by unit tests.
  • Update documentation if you change CLI behavior.

docs.title: Pull Request Process
docs.text:
1. Create a new branch for your feature or bugfix.
  2. Commit your changes with descriptive messages.
  3. Submit a PR and describe the impact of your changes.
  4. Ensure all CI checks pass.

docs.title: License
docs.text: TestForge is licensed under the Apache 2.0 License.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python >= 3.10
  • Git

docs.title: Install from Source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/BMike10/testforge/
cd testforge
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -e .

docs.title: Environment Configuration
docs.text: TestForge is model-agnostic and works with both cloud-based LLMs (OpenAI, Anthropic) and local-first models (via vLLM, LM Studio, Ollama).

docs.title: Local LLM Setup (Recommended)
docs.text:
Local models like Qwen-2.5-Coder or DeepSeek-V3 offer high performance with strict privacy.

Create a .env file in your project root:

# Local API Base (LM Studio, vLLM, etc.)
export LLM_API_BASE="http://127.0.0.1:1234/v1"

# Default Model name
export LLM_MODEL_NAME="mistralai/ministral-3-14b-reasoning"

# API Key (use 'no-key' for local servers)
export LLM_API_KEY="no-key"

docs.title: Cloud LLM Setup
docs.text:
For OpenAI or other providers, simply provide the API key and model name.

export LLM_MODEL_NAME="gpt-4o"
export LLM_API_KEY="sk-..."

docs.title: Quick Start in 3 Steps
docs.text:
1. Map your project: 
    testforge map src/
  2. Plan tests for a module: 
    testforge plan src/core/logic.py
  3. Generate and validate: 
    testforge generate src/core/logic.py

Ready for more? Check out the CLI Reference.

docs.title: Scientific Core & Methodology 🔬
docs.text: TestForge is built on rigorous computer science principles to ensure that AI-generated tests are not just "hallucinations" of code, but valid engineering artifacts.

docs.title: 1. Intelligent Module Filtering
docs.text:
The Problem: Codebases are often cluttered with utility files, empty __init__.py modules, or data-only files that don't contain testable logic. Sending these to an LLM wastes tokens and generates noisy, low-value tests.

The Solution: TestForge employs a deterministic AST Heuristic to identify "testable" modules.

Under the Hood: Filtering Logic

When the Orchestrator scans your project, it invokes ContextManager.analyze_file() which uses the following logic: 1. AST Parsing: The file is parsed into an Abstract Syntax Tree using ast. 2. Symbol Extraction: We count the number of ClassDef and FunctionDef nodes. 3. Testability Flag: A module is marked is_testable = True only if it contains at least one class or one function. 4. Skipping: Files like empty __init__.py or global constant files are automatically identified as is_testable = False and moved to the "Skipped" list in the pipeline output.

docs.title: 2. Topological Dependency Resolution
docs.text:
The Problem: Testing a high-level module (e.g., a CLI wrapper) before its core logic dependencies leads to "context bloat" and circular reasoning in AI prompts.

The Solution: TestForge builds a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of your entire codebase.

graph TD
    Utils[utils/env_setup.py] --> Core[core/context.py]
    Core --> Agent[agents/coder.py]
    Agent --> Orchestrator[core/orchestrator.py]
    Orchestrator --> CLI[cli.py]

Under the Hood: Graph Resolution

  1. Import Scanning: We use AST to extract all internal import and from ... import statements.
  2. DAG Construction: Using networkx, we map every module as a node and every import as a directed edge (dependency -> dependent).
  3. Bottom-Up Sort: We apply a Topological Sort. This ensures that "leaf" modules (those with 0 dependencies) are processed first.
  4. Verified Context: By the time the AI reaches a high-level module, its dependencies have already been "validated" by the pipeline.

docs.title: 3. McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity
docs.text:
The Problem: Simple "getter" methods don't require the same level of architectural rigor as complex nested algorithms.

The Solution: We calculate the complexity score ($M = E - N + 2P$) for every symbol.

Under the Hood: Complexity Calculation

TestForge calculates complexity during the deterministic analysis phase: * Base Score: Every function starts with a complexity of 1. * Decision Points: We increment the score for every if, while, for, and, or, and except block found in the AST. * Risk Prioritization: High-complexity modules are flagged in the execution_plan.yaml and the AI is explicitly prompted to use Boundary Value Analysis to cover every logical branch.

docs.title: 4. Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) & DSE Simulation
docs.text:
The Problem: AI often generates "happy path" inputs and misses edge cases (e.g., empty lists, None values, or integer overflows).

The Solution: TestForge forces the AI to simulate a Dynamic Symbolic Execution (DSE) engine.

  • Equivalence Partitioning: The AI must define "Safe," "Boundary," and "Illegal" input ranges.
  • Simulated Execution: The prompt template (plan_tests.j2) requires the AI to trace every logical path identified in the McCabe analysis and design inputs that specifically trigger those paths.

docs.title: 5. Mutation-Guided Hardening
docs.text:
The Problem: 100% line coverage is a "vanity metric." You can execute a line without actually asserting its behavior is correct.

The Solution: We use Fault Injection via mutmut to prove test rigor.

The Mutant Test

  1. Injection: mutmut modifies your source code (e.g., changing > to >=).
  2. Execution: Your generated tests are run against the "mutant."
  3. Survival Check: If the tests still pass, the mutant "survived," meaning your assertions are weak.
  4. Autonomous Healing: TestForge identifies the survival, feeds the mutant type back to the AI, and demands a stronger assertion to "kill" it.

docs.title: 🛠️ TestForge: Scientifically Validated AI Test Generation
docs.text:
TestForge is a high-integrity CLI framework that combines Deterministic Static Analysis with Agentic AI (powered by Aider) to generate, validate, and harden Python unit test suites.

Moving beyond simple line coverage and standard AI code generation, TestForge employs topological mapping, complexity metrics, simulated symbolic execution, and mutation testing to ensure generated tests are structurally sound, behaviorally rigorous, and enterprise-ready.

docs.title: 🔬 Scientific Core & Methodology
docs.text:
TestForge operates on a continuous Research -> Plan -> Generate -> Validate lifecycle, strictly grounded in computer science principles:

  1. Topological Dependency Resolution (Bottom-Up)
    • Concept: Builds a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of the codebase using Abstract Syntax Trees (AST).
    • Execution: Tests "leaf" modules (components with no dependencies) first. When testing high-level modules, the AI knows exactly what to mock based on the dependency graph, preventing "context bloat" and circular reasoning.
  2. McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity & Risk Prioritization
    • Concept: Deterministically calculates the complexity of every function/class using the graph-theoretic formula $M = E - N + 2P$.
    • Execution: The AI is explicitly instructed to prioritize high-complexity paths, ensuring the most fragile and dangerous code receives the most rigorous testing.
  3. Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) & DSE Simulation
    • Concept: Prevents the AI from "guessing" superficial test inputs.
    • Execution: TestForge forces the AI to define equivalence classes and explicitly test the $n-1$, $n$, and $n+1$ boundaries. Furthermore, it prompts the AI to simulate Dynamic Symbolic Execution (DSE) by mapping out logical branches and explicitly generating inputs to satisfy each unique path.
  4. Mutation-Guided Hardening
    • Concept: Line coverage is a "weak" metric. TestForge uses mutmut to inject semantic faults (mutants) into the source code.
    • Execution: If a test suite passes but a mutant survives, the test is empirically weak. TestForge identifies the surviving mutant and autonomously tasks the AI with hardening the assertions to "kill" it.

docs.title: ⚙️ Enterprise Configuration & Dynamic Routing
docs.text:
TestForge is built to adapt to your company's specific engineering standards and infrastructure limits. It is highly configurable and model-agnostic.

  • Dynamic Model Routing: You are not locked into a single LLM for the entire pipeline. TestForge allows you to route different phases to different models. For example, you can use a fast, cost-effective model (or a local model) for Phase 1 (Architectural Scanning) and switch to a heavy reasoning model for Phase 3 (Test Generation).
  • Template-Driven Configuration: Every phase and LLM interaction is based on explicit prompt templates. Developers can easily override these templates to enforce internal coding guidelines, specific mocking libraries, or custom assertions.
  • Transparent Variable Injection: All variables available to the prompt templates (e.g., {{ file_path }}, {{ ast_complexity_score }}, {{ dependency_graph }}) are strictly documented in the Developer Guide.
  • Strict Privacy Mode (Local LLMs): Natively supports local inference via Ollama or vLLM. Enterprises can run architectural scanning entirely on local hardware to prevent proprietary IP from leaving the network.

docs.title: 🛡️ System Safety & State Management
docs.text:
• Agentic Context Isolation: Prevents LLM hallucination through strict memory management. The AI only "sees" the target file, the project's architecture map, and a deterministic summary of its immediate dependencies.
  • The Execution Ledger (State Checkpointing): TestForge maintains a strict pipeline plan saved in a cached state file. If the CLI is interrupted, hits an API error, or is paused by the user, it can resume exactly where it left off without duplicating work or API costs.
  • Financial Safety & Token Management: * Calculates the DAG, counts tokens, and provides an estimated API cost via a --dry-run command before execution.
    • Implements a strict max_retries circuit breaker. If a test fails after $X$ attempts, it is logged and bypassed, preventing infinite loop API billing.
  • Fixture Isolation (The conftest.py Shield): The AI is strictly prohibited from editing global conftest.py files during active unit testing to prevent a "global blast radius." Instead, it generates local fixtures. A separate testforge refactor-fixtures command safely elevates duplicated local fixtures to the global conftest.py.

docs.title: Phase 1: Architectural Topography & Documentation
docs.text:
Before writing tests, TestForge builds a complete mental model of the codebase.

  1. Project Architecture Generation:
    • Deterministic Scan: Uses Python AST tools to map classes, functions, and imports.
    • Simple Mode: Combines static analysis with a quick LLM scan to generate a high-level project map.
    • Complete Mode (Iterative): Iterating file-by-file (skipping utilities like __init__.py), it uses the AI to document specific modules deeply. This layered approach prevents overwhelming the LLM's context window.
  2. Codebase Documentation (Optional/Pre-requisite):
    • Analyzes the requested codebase to write or improve documentation (e.g., Google Docstrings). Generates an intermediate "Doc-Plan" for human review before applying changes.

docs.title: Phase 2: Strategic Test Planning
docs.text:
With the map established, TestForge creates a deterministic battle plan. Note: All plans generated in this phase are output as markdown files. TestForge can be configured to block execution here, allowing the developer to review and manually edit the AI's strategy before generating code.

  1. Global Suite Strategy: Generates a comprehensive plan detailing how the codebase will be tested, identifying critical paths, maximum complexity areas, and shared fixture strategies.
  2. Unit Strategy (Micro-Plans): Generates a specific, markdown-based strategy file for an individual module or file. This acts as the strict blueprint the AI must follow when generating code.

docs.title: Phase 3: Agentic Execution & Validation Loop
docs.text:
The active generation phase where tests are written, run, and iteratively fixed based on the Phase 2 strategy.

  • Execution Modes:
    • Single-File Mode: Executes the unit strategy for one specific file using HitL (Human-in-the-Loop) pauses for review.
    • Suite Iteration Mode: Uses the Global Suite Strategy to systematically iterate over the entire codebase.
    • CI/CD Headless Mode: Built for GitHub Actions/Jenkins. When triggered by a PR, TestForge analyzes the diff, generates tests only for changed files and their DAG dependents, and pushes a commit directly without human intervention.
  • The Validation Loop (Self-Healing):
    1. AI generates test -> pytest executed -> Coverage/Mutation checked.
    2. If the test fails, errors out, or hallucinates, the error traceback is fed back to the AI alongside the original strategy.
    3. The AI corrects the test. This loops until the test passes perfectly or hits the circuit breaker.
